coredata.dc:title: A facile and sensitive ratiometric fluorescent sensor for determination of gallic acid
coredata.dc:creator: Tan, Qin
An, Xuanxuan
Pan, Shuang
Zhen, Shujun
Hu, Yongmei
Hu, Xiaoli
coredata.dc:description: Gallic acid (GA), which is widely found in some tea leaves, fruits and medicinal materials, has a variety of biological activities, including anti-oxidation, anti-cancer, anti-bacterial. Thus, monitoring the amount of GA is of great significance. We developed a GA ratiometric fluorescent sensor by carbon dots (CDs), CoOOH nanoflakes and o-phenylenediamine (OPD). In the absence of GA, OPD can be oxidized to 2,3-diaminophenazine (DAP) by CoOOH nanoflakes. DAP not only quenches the fluorescence of CDs at 450 nm through the inner filter effect (IFE), but also produces a new fluorescence emission peak at 570 nm. However, the presence of GA is able to cause collapse of CoOOH nanoflakes which are reduced to Co2+, thus impeding the formation of DAP. Based on this principle, ratiometric detection of GA is achieved. The novel ratiometric fluorescent sensor shows high sensitivity toward GA and the linear response is observed in the 2.0–60.0 μmol L−1 with a detection limit of 1.2 μmol L−1. Furthermore, the CDs/CoOOH nanoflakes/OPD sensor is applied to the detection of GA in green tea.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ratiometric fluorescence sensor
Gallic acid
Carbon dots
CoOOH nanoflakes

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Procedure for the assay of GA
Possible mechanism for ratiometric determination of GA

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image of CoOOH nanoflakes. (B) XRD of CoOOH nanoflakes. (C) FTIR spectrum of CoOOH nanoflakes. (D) XPS spectra of CoOOH nanoflakes. (E) High resolution spectra of Co 2p.

(A) Ratiometric fluorescence spectra of different concentrations of GA from 2.0 to 60.0 μmol L-1. (B) Linear relationship of F 450/F 570 with concentrations of GA from 2.0 to 10.0 μmol L-1 (blue line); Linear relationship of F 450/F 570 with concentrations of GA from 10.0 to 60.0 μmol L-1 (red line). Conditions: V CDs = 50.0 μL, λ ex = 402 nm, pH 5.0, V CoOOH = 200.0 μL, Reaction time = 80 min. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(A) Fluorescence spectra of CDs, CDs + GA, CDs + OPD, CDs + CoOOH + OPD and CDs + CoOOH + OPD + GA. (B) UV–vis spectra of CDs, OPD + CoOOH and CDs + OPD + CoOOH. (C) UV–vis spectra (dash line) of OPD + CoOOH and OPD + CoOOH + GA, and fluorescence excitation, emission spectra (solid line) of CDs. (D) Fluorescence decay curves of CDs and CDs + DAP.
Schematic illustration of ratiometric fluorescence sensing for GA.
Determination of GA in green tea using ratiometric fluorescence method and HPLC (n = 5)

head.ce:title: A facile and sensitive ratiometric fluorescent sensor for determination of gallic acid

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Luminescence Analysis and Molecular Sensing, Ministry of Education, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Luminescence Analysis and Molecular Sensing
College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Luminescence Analysis and Molecular Sensing, Ministry of Education, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Chengdu Second People’s Hospital, Chengdu 610017, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Chengdu Second People’s Hospital, Chengdu 610017, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Gallic acid (GA), which is widely found in some tea leaves, fruits and medicinal materials, has a variety of biological activities, including anti-oxidation, anti-cancer, anti-bacterial. Thus, monitoring the amount of GA is of great significance. We developed a GA ratiometric fluorescent sensor by carbon dots (CDs), CoOOH nanoflakes and o-phenylenediamine (OPD). In the absence of GA, OPD can be oxidized to 2,3-diaminophenazine (DAP) by CoOOH nanoflakes. DAP not only quenches the fluorescence of CDs at 450 nm through the inner filter effect (IFE), but also produces a new fluorescence emission peak at 570 nm. However, the presence of GA is able to cause collapse of CoOOH nanoflakes which are reduced to Co2+, thus impeding the formation of DAP. Based on this principle, ratiometric detection of GA is achieved. The novel ratiometric fluorescent sensor shows high sensitivity toward GA and the linear response is observed in the 2.0–60.0 μmol L−1 with a detection limit of 1.2 μmol L−1. Furthermore, the CDs/CoOOH nanoflakes/OPD sensor is applied to the detection of GA in green tea.

ce:section.ce:para: Gallic acid (GA) is a kind of organic acid which widely exists in many plants like tea leaves, some medicinal materials (rhubarb, gallnuts and big leafhopper) and fruits (grape, berries and pomegranate) [1–3]. Several research indicates that as an antioxidant, GA has the ability to scavenge free radicals [4–6]. In addition, GA has been shown to a variety of biological activities like anti-inflammatory, anti-bacterial, anti-mutagenic, antihistaminic and antitumor activities [7–10]. Therefore, GA has been widely used in food, biology, medicine and other fields. However, as an aromatic compound, GA is difficult to be biodegraded and long-term accumulation in vivo will cause harm to the human body [11]. In recent years, GA has been found to be illegally added to some food, beverages and health-care products, so the determination of GA is crucial for human health [12,13]. Up to now, various methods have been established to detect GA including chromatography [14], capillary electrophoresis [15], colorimetric methods [16], electrochemical methods [17], chemiluminescence [18], fluorescence spectrometry [19] and molecularly imprinted methods [20]. Among all those methods, fluorescence methods exhibit significant superiorities, such as simple, effective, comparatively low-cost and simple operation. Consequently, fluorescence method it is crucial to build a fluorescent sensor to accurately quantify GA.

Fluorescent sensors have been favored by many researchers in analysis and detection because of their advantages such as fast analysis speed, high sensitivity, low cost and simple equipment. However, most fluorescent sensors rely on a single signal. Single signal fluorescent sensor is susceptible to many factors such as concentration of sensor, excitation intensity, measurement condition, environmental noises and light source [21]. Interestingly, ratiometric fluorescent sensor can provide two or more fluorescence signals, which one of the signals acts as a built-in correction system [22,23]. Moreover, ratiometric fluorescent sensor can provide a background signal that is not affected by ambient noise and light sources [24,25]. At present time, most ratiometric fluorescent sensors are composed of quantum dots, metal nanoclusters, organic dyes or carbon nanomaterials [26–30]. For example, Li et al. (2020) combined CdTe quantum dots in an imprinted shell to construct a molecularly imprinted ratiometric fluorescence sensor for determination of folic acid [31]. Yang et al. (2020) established a multi-emitting (blue, green, and red) ratiometric fluorescence sensor which was obtained by encapsulating green and red fluorescent quantum dots on SiO2 cores and mixing them in appropriate proportions [32]. But in all of these materials, quantum dots may contain heavy metal elements such as cadmium and selenium. Thus, inherent toxicity and scintillation limit their application. Organic dyes have the disadvantages of easy photobleaching, low quantum yield and narrow excitation range. Carbon dots (CDs), as an environmentally friendly burgeoning carbon nanomaterial, have excellent photostability, water dispersibility, tunable emission capability and biological compatibility [33–36]. CDs have broad application prospects in catalysis, photodynamic therapy, biological labeling, and drug delivery [37,38]. Until now, CDs as a ratiometric fluorescent sensor have been widely used. For example, Jiao et al. (2019) used nitrogen-doped CDs for ratiometric detection of Ag+ and glutathione [39]. Gogoi et al. (2019) developed a ratiometric fluorescence sensor base on CDs and graphene quantum dots detection of As (III) [40]. Therefore, because of the above unique advantages and promising applications, CDs are very suitable for the preparation of ratiometric fluorescence sensor.

Herein, a facile ratiometric fluorescence method for gallic acid (GA) assay was established by CDs/CoOOH nanoflakes/ o-phenylenediamine (OPD) sensor (Scheme 1 ). As emerging 2D nanomaterials, CoOOH nanoflakes had excellent oxidizability, water dispersibility and the preparation procedure was simple and practicable. CoOOH nanoflakes oxidized OPD to 2,3-diaminophenazine (DAP) resulting in a new fluorescence emission peak at 570 nm, while the fluorescence of CDs at 450 nm was quenched by DAP because of inner filter effect (IFE). The introduction of GA reduced CoOOH nanoflakes to Co2+. The decomposition and collapse of the CoOOH nanoflakes reduced the generation of DAP, which caused IFE being inhibited. Therefore, the ratiometric detection of GA was achieved and successfully applied to the determination of GA in green tea.

All the reagents were analytical reagent. All the reagents were used as received without further purification and the water used in the experiments was deionized water. ZnCl2, CaCl2, MgCl2, KCl, NaCl, MnCl2, FeCl3 and octadecylsilane chemically bonded silica were purchased from Macklin Biochemical (Shanghai, China). OPD, GA, N-ethylmaleimide (NEM), DAP, glucose, sucrose, l-cysteine, serine, glycine, arginine, leucine, l-glutathione, sarcosine and glutamate were obtained from Aladdin Biochemical (Shanghai, China). Sodium hydroxide, ammonium thiocyanate, ammonium citrate, quinine sulfate, phosphoric acid and methanol were bought from Oriental Reagent Factory (Sichuan, China). Cobalt sulfate heptahydrate, phosphoric acid, acetic acid, sodium hypochlorite and boric acid were supplied by Kelong Chemical Reagent Factory (Chengdu, China). The green tea (Chongqing Yulin Tea Co., LTD) was obtained from the Yonghui supermarket (Chongqing, China). Dialysis membrane was bought from Yi Bo Biological (USA).

Fluorescence spectrum was recorded by Hitachi fluorescence spectrophotometer (Tokyo, Japan, F-2500). UV absorption spectrum was obtained by Hitachi UV–vis spectrophotometer (Tokyo, Japan, UV-2450). Infrared spectrometer (FTIR, Tokyo, Japan, FTIR-8400S) was used to explore the functional groups. The morphology and the size of the as obtained materials were studied by transmission electron microscopy (TEM, FEI, Talos, F200X). X-ray photoelectron spectroscope (XPS, Thermo Scientific Escalab, USA, 250Xi) was used to analyze the elemental composition, chemical state and molecular structure of materials. The composition of materials was obtained by X-Ray diffractometer (XRD, Bruker, Germany, AXS-D8). The content of samples was determined by high performance liquid chromatograph (HPLC, Hitachi, Tokyo, Japan, D-7000).

CoOOH nanoflakes were synthesized according to a previous procedure with some modifications [41]. Briefly, 3.0 mL 1.0 mol L-1of NaOH solution was mixed with 100.0 mL 10.0 mmol L-1 of CoSO4 solution, and the mixture was sonicated for 10 min. After that, the resulting solution was centrifuged at 12,000 rpm for 10 min. The resulting sediment was re-dispersed with 100.0 mL of deionized water. Then, 2.6 mL 1.7 mol L-1 of NaClO was added to the above solution and ultrasonication for 10 min. After this step, the product was obtained by centrifuging and washing four times with deionized water and dried at 60 °C. Finally, the dry CoOOH powder was dispersed in water to obtain a solution concentration of 0.2 mg mL−1.

0.2 g of ammonium thiocyanate and 0.4 g of ammonium citrate were mixed with 15.0 mL of deionized water. The mixed solutions were heated in a Teflon-lined autoclave 160 °C for 6 h. After cooling naturally, the resulting solution was centrifuged at 12,000 rpm for 10 min to remove large particles. Finally, the CDs were obtained by dialysis membrane (1,000 Da) purifying for 12 h for future use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Procedure for the assay of GA
ce:section.ce:para: Britton-Robinson buffer (BR) (200.0 µL, 4.0 mmol L -1, pH 5.0), CoOOH nanoflakes (200.0 µL, 0.2 mg mL−1), varying concentrations of GA (100.0 µL, 2.0–60.0 μmol L-1) and 600.0 µL of deionized water were mixed. Then, 200.0 µL of OPD (10.0 mmol L-1) was added to the mixtures for 80 min at room temperature. After that, 50.0 µL of CDs was added to the mixtures. Finally, the fluorescence spectra were recorded. The slit widths maintained at 10 nm and the excitation wavelength remained at 402 nm throughout the experiment.

Green tea (1.0 g) was soaked with boiling distilled water (20.0 mL) for three times then filtered with 0.22 μm filter membranes and then was mixed with 0.1 mL of standard GA solution at different concentrations (67.5, 135.0, 202.5 mmol L−1). The sample solution was transferred the filtrate to a 100.0 mL volumetric flask and diluted to the scale line. Finally, GA in the real samples was detected by the developed method described above. In the meantime, Green tea (1.0 g) was dissolved with mobile phase (0.05 % phosphoric acid solution - methanol (93:7), 20.0 mL) for three times then filtered with 0.45 μm filter membranes. The filtrate obtained was mixed with 0.1 mL of standard GA solution at different concentrations (67.5, 135.0, 202.5 mmol L−1) and then was diluted using mobile phase to the scale line in a 100.0 mL volumetric flask. Finally, GA was quantitatively analyzed by HPLC of Chinese Pharmacopoeia (2015, 4th part, page 514) [54].

The hydrothermal method was widely used in the preparation of CDs. Hydrothermal method refers to the general term of chemical reaction carried out in aqueous solution or other fluids under certain temperature and pressure. The synthesis method greatly simplified the synthetic procedure by avoiding the complicated multi-step preparation. More importantly, the CDs can provide one of fluorescence signals in ratiometric fluorescent sensor. Moreover, OPD had no effect on the fluorescence intensity of CDs. However, CoOOH nanoflakes can oxidize OPD to DAP because of oxidizability. DAP not only generated another one fluorescence signal but also quenched the fluorescence of CDs. To sum up, we selected three materials (CDs, CoOOH nanoflakes and OPD) to construct a ratiometric fluorescent sensor.

TEM image (Fig. S1A) of CDs illustrated spherical morphology and uniform particle size distribution. In addition, CDs displayed an average diameter of 2.22 nm in the range of 1.13 nm to 3.80 nm (an inset of Fig. S1A). In XRD pattern (Fig. S1B), a broad peak at 21.7° verified disordered graphitized structure of CDs. In Fig. S1C (FT-IR spectrum of CDs), the peak around 3415 cm−1 was ascribed to the stretching vibrations of O–H [42]. The peak located at 2030 cm−1 belonged to C-N bending vibration [43]. The peak at 1629 cm−1 was the stretching vibrations of C = O [44]. XPS spectrum (Fig. S1D) of CDs exhibited the existence of C, N and O. The three peaks at 288.7 eV, 285.4 eV and 284.8 eV in the high resolution spectra of C 1 s (Fig. S1E) belonged to the C = O, C-O/C-N and C-O/C = C bonds, severally [43], which consistent with the FT-IR spectrum of CDs. In the high resolution spectra of N 1 s (Fig. S1F), three nitrogen species appear including graphitic N at 401.8 eV, pyridinic N at 400.1 eV and pyrrolic N at 398.1 eV [44]. Graphitic N indicated that a part of N atoms was doped into the carbon framework. Pyridinic N and pyrrolic N showed that the rest of N atoms exist in the form of amino groups [43].

TEM image (Fig. 1 A) clearly revealed that the morphology of a two-dimensional sheet of CoOOH nanoflakes structure was agreed with previously reported in the literature. As displayed in XRD pattern (Fig. 1B), three diffraction peaks at 2θ = 20.0°, 2θ = 38.0° and 2θ = 51.0° corresponded three lattice faces ((003), (012) and (015)) of CoOOH nanoflakes, respectively [41]. Fig. 1C presented FT-IR spectrum of CoOOH nanoflakes. The broad absorption band centered at 3425 cm−1 was assigned to the stretching of –OH. Characteristic absorption peaks of the Co-O and Co-O 2- bond were observed at 1620 cm−1 and 586 cm−1, respectively [45]. XPS survey of CoOOH nanoflakes was shown in Fig. 1D and E. Two peaks at 795.4 eV and 780.4 eV belonged to Co 2p1/2 and Co 2p3/2, which indicated the oxidation state of cobalt is Co (III) in the CoOOH nanoflakes [46].

In Fig. S2A, the position of the maximum emission peak was constant under different excitation (340 nm − 420 nm), which demonstrated the surface of the CDs was uniform. The maximum emission wavelength of CDs was around 440 nm by maximum excitation at 360 nm. Fig. S2B showed a broad UV absorption band at 345 nm was attributed to the n − π* transition of the C = O bond [44]. In addition, although the maximum excitation wavelength of CDs was 360 nm, the maximum excitation wavelength of DAP was around 400 nm. If 360 nm is chosen as the excitation wavelength, this will cause the peak of DAP to be absorbed and masked. When 402 nm was selected as the excitation wavelength, not only was the peak of CDs obvious, but also the peak of DAP will not be absorbed and masked. Therefore, 402 nm was selected as the excitation wavelength in order to realize the ratiometric detection of GA.

For obtaining the best detection performance, we optimized these experimental parameters (pH, reaction time and the volume of CoOOH nanoflakes). In Fig. S3A, ΔF 450/F 570 (the difference of ratiometric fluorescence with GA and without GA) reached the highest value at pH 5.0. Therefore, pH 5.0 was chosen for GA detection. The value of F 450/F 570 nearly remained constant after 80 min of reaction time in Fig. S3B. Thus, 80 min was the optimal incubation time for GA detection. Fig. S3C clearly exhibited that ΔF 450/F 570 was maximum when the volume of CoOOH nanoflakes was 200.0 μL.

Under the optimal experiment conditions, opposite changes (F 450 gradually increased while F 570 slowly decreased) was observed in Fig. 2 A with the increasing concentration of GA. The relationship of ratiometric fluorescence intensity (F 450/F 570) and the concentration of GA was depicted in Fig. 2B. The regression equations were F 450/F 570 = 0.03322c + 0.3528 (R2 = 0.9995) and F 450/F 570 = 0.1130c-0.5486 (R2 = 0.9932) with the GA concentration in the range of 2.0–10.0 μmol L−1 and 10.0–60.0 μmol L−1, respectively. The limit of detection (LOD) was obtained to be 1.2 μmol L−1 on the basis of the 3σ/k (σ was the standard deviation of eleven blank samples, and k was the slope) principle. Furthermore, we compared this sensor with previous sensors for GA detection (Table 1 ). Compared with colorimetry and chemiluminescence in Table 1, the CDs/CoOOH nanoflakes/OPD sensor had a wider linear range. As can be seen in Table 1, this sensor offers lower LOD and simple operation manners compared to electrochemical detection methods. In addition, compared with these proposed single-signal sensors, the dual-signal of CDs/CoOOH nanoflakes/OPD sensor was able to reduce or eliminate the errors caused by environmental fluctuations and provide self-calibration.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible mechanism for ratiometric determination of GA
ce:section.ce:para: As depicted in Fig. 3 A, fluorescence intensity of the CDs was almost unaffected by OPD or GA, respectively. In the coexistence of OPD and CoOOH nanoflakes, OPD was transformed into DAP by CoOOH nanoflakes, which produced a new emission at 570 nm. Meanwhile, emission of CDs at 450 nm was quenched. However, emission of CDs at 450 nm recovered while the new emission at 570 nm decreased after adding GA, indicating that GA was decrease the generation of DAP. To further verify the sensing mechanism, UV–vis absorption spectra of CDs, OPD + CoOOH and CDs + OPD + CoOOH were recorded in Fig. 3B. The position of UV–vis absorption peak of CDs no movement was observed in the presence of OPD + CoOOH, which meant that OPD + CoOOH did not react with CDs. As shown in Fig. 3C, UV–vis absorption peak intensity of OPD + CoOOH was a clearly decline in the presence of GA, which also indicated GA effectively prevented OPD from being transformed into DAP [52]. Furthermore, Fig. 3C showed that both excitation and emission of CDs were partially overlap with UV–vis absorption spectra of OPD + CoOOH, which made IFE was deemed to be a possible quenching mechanism [53]. Fluorescence lifetime was an effective parameter to verify IFE. Fig. 3D illustrated the fluorescence lifetimes of the CDs before (6.84 ns) and after adding DAP (6.83 ns). The results confirmed that fluorescence quenching mechanism between CDs and DAP was attributed to IFE [53].

We analyzed and tested a series of potential interference substances including ions (Zn2+, Ca2+, Mg2+, K+, Na+, Mn2+, Fe3+, Co2+) saccharides (glucose, sucrose), ascorbic acid, amino acid (arginine, glutamate, serine, sarcosine, glycine, leucine, l-glutathione, l-cysteine) and phenols (catechol, salicylic acid and phenol). The concentration of GA was 50.0 μmol L−1, and the concentrations of other potential interference substances were 500.0 μmol L−1 except ascorbic acid, l-glutathione and l-cysteine. The sensor manifested high selectivity toward GA determination in Fig. S4A and B. Although l-glutathione and l-cysteine exhibited influence on the ratiometric fluorescent sensor, sulfhydryl groups of l-glutathione and l-cysteine can react with NEM. As illustrated in Fig. S4B, there was a negligible influence on the ratiometric system after incubation of l-glutathione and l-cysteine with NEM. Moreover, NEM did not interfere with the measurement of GA. Thus, the ratiometric fluorescent sensor had good selectivity and high anti-interference for GA detection.

In order to verify the feasibility and accuracy of the ratiometric fluorescence sensor for GA determination in green tea, real samples were taken to do the recovery experiments using this method and HPLC of Chinese Pharmacopoeia (2015, 4th part, page 514) [54], respectively. Preparation of real samples was explained in the previous section. Correlated results were listed in Table 2 . The results acquired by the developed method and HPLC were in accord. The recovery was calculated to be 97.3% to 106.0% and relative standard deviation (RSD) ranges from 1.5% to 3.1%. These results demonstrated the accuracy and reliability of the ratiometric fluorescent sensor for GA determination in real samples and its great potential for practical analysis.

In this work, a facile and sensitive ratiometric fluorescent sensor for GA determination based on CDs/CoOOH nanoflakes/OPD system was constructed. The proposed ratiometric fluorescent sensor displayed good selectivity to GA and then was applied to the determination of GA in green tea with satisfactory results. Moreover, the sample throughput of this method was incubated for 80 min at room temperature. In many analytical laboratories, whilst maintaining the original method performance, options to increase sample throughput for analyses are highly desirable. Therefore, attempts to increase the sample throughput of this method are in progress. In addition, in order to verify the efficiency and accuracy of the developed method, Chinese pharmacopoeia method (0512 HPLC) as the standard method was carried out to validate the newly developed method. The results indicate that the developed method is a practical, reliable and universal method.
Qin Tan: Writing – original draft. Xuanxuan An: Data curation. Shuang Pan: Investigation. Shujun Zhen: Data curation. Yongmei Hu: Supervision. Xiaoli Hu: .

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge financial support of the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21974109) and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (No. XDJK2019TY003).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2021.106922.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
